title: Media conference: Monetary Policy Decision – 9 December 2025
summary: The RBA Board left the cash rate unchanged at 3.6% on December 9, 2025, with no rate cut considered at the meeting. Governor Michele Bullock noted that September quarter inflation came in stronger than expected with signs of persistence, tilting the balance of risks to the upside. The Board discussed circumstances under which rate hikes might be needed if inflation proves persistent and does not move back toward the 2.5% target.
key_points: RBA held cash rate at 3.6% on December 9, 2025
No rate cut was on the table; Board did not explicitly consider a hike but discussed circumstances for future increases
September quarter inflation was stronger than expected with some signs of persistence in certain items
Balance of risks to inflation has tilted to the upside; downside risks from overseas have abated
Headline inflation at 3.8% versus 2.5% target; underlying momentum in private economy recovering
Electricity rebates temporarily lower headline inflation and Board will look through their effects as they roll off
Labor market remains a bit tight; private demand growth was stronger than expected, driven by business investment
why_it_matters: The RBA's decision to hold rates signals a cautious approach while acknowledging upside inflation risks. This sets the stage for potential future rate hikes if inflation persistence is confirmed in upcoming data. Markets and analysts closely watch RBA communications for signals on the direction of Australian monetary policy, which affects AUD, Australian bonds, and broader risk sentiment.
macro_context: The RBA is balancing returning inflation to its 2.5% target against maintaining low unemployment. The private economy is recovering and taking over from public demand. GDP growth was broadly in line with November forecasts, but underlying inflation momentum remains elevated. The Board will assess new labor market and inflation data, including quarterly trimmed mean, ahead of the February meeting.
important_numbers: 0.8% Q on Q inflation forecast mentioned for Q1

human_review.notes: Historical record (112 days old as of 2026-03-31). Truncated transcript may have omitted relevant policy discussion. Confidence slightly lowered due to incomplete source text.
